title: What is self-compassion?
citation: Neff, K.D. (2003). The development and validation of a scale to measure self-compassion. Self and Identity, 2(3), 223-250.
content:
Self-compassion, as defined by psychologist Kristin Neff, is the practice of turning towards oneself with the same kindness and understanding that one might offer a good friend who is struggling. Neff's model identifies three interacting components that together constitute self-compassion.

The first component is mindfulness, which involves noticing painful thoughts and feelings as they are, without suppressing them or becoming overwhelmed by them. Without this quality of open awareness, it is difficult to respond compassionately to one's own distress, because the distress has either been pushed away or amplified into an all-consuming crisis. The second component is common humanity, which involves recognising that suffering, failure, and personal inadequacy are part of the shared human experience rather than something that singles an individual out as uniquely deficient. When people feel alone in their difficulties, self-criticism tends to intensify. Recognising that imperfection and struggle are universal can ease that isolation. The third component is self-kindness, which involves responding to oneself with warmth and care rather than harsh judgment or contempt.

Self-compassion is sometimes confused with self-pity, the tendency to become absorbed in one's own difficulties and to feel cut off from others. The difference is significant: self-compassion involves connecting to shared human experience, while self-pity tends to amplify a sense of isolation. Self-compassion is also distinct from self-esteem, which depends on evaluating oneself positively and can fluctuate sharply with performance and social comparison. Self-compassion does not require a positive self-evaluation; it is available regardless of whether things are going well.

Research by Neff and colleagues has consistently found that self-compassion is associated with greater emotional resilience, lower levels of anxiety and depression, and a reduced tendency to ruminate. It supports the capacity to acknowledge mistakes without being derailed by them, which in turn makes it easier to learn from setbacks and to persist through difficulty.
